title: How Anthropic Engineers ACTUALLY Prompt Claude Code
summary: Austin Marchese outlines four rules for prompting Claude Code derived from studying Anthropic engineers' actual practices. The rules emphasize prompting for skills rather than asking Claude directly, composability, persistent skill development, and controlling invocation scope. The patterns suggest saving scripts within skills and managing access control.
writeup: Drawing on Anthropic engineering presentations and the AI Code Summit, this video distills four rules for how Anthropic's own engineers prompt Claude Code. The central shift is from writing one-off prompts to building reusable skills: organized file collections holding a description, step-by-step instructions, and tools (scripts, API calls, reference files). Composability and iterative refinement are the through-line: small, focused skills that chain together and sharpen with each session outperform monolithic prompts that degrade the moment a chat closes.
key_points: Skills in Claude Code are folders with three layers: a description (tells Claude when to invoke the skill), instructions (the step-by-step playbook), and tools (code scripts, API calls, reference files); most users stop at layer two and neglect the tools layer where Anthropic engineers say the real leverage lives.

Saving a deterministic script inside a skill trades expensive, variable AI token generation for cheap, repeatable code compute: Barry's team stopped letting Claude rewrite the same Python slide-styling script each session and instead saved it as a tool inside the skill folder.

Two invocation flags control who can trigger a skill: setting user_invocable to false hides a skill from the slash menu so only agents can invoke it, while disable_model_invocation reserves a skill for human-only use, which is appropriate for high-risk actions like sending messages or deploying code to production.

Composable skills (small, single-purpose, reusable) outperform monolithic skills because a focused skill is easier to debug, improvements propagate automatically to every workflow that uses it, and individual skills can be reused across entirely different pipelines without rebuilding.

Skills accumulate value across sessions: unlike a prompt that is lost when the chat closes, a skill persists and can be sharpened on every use, so Anthropic's framing is that Claude on day 30 is materially better than Claude on day 1 because the skill layer encodes learned working patterns.
